title: Bastarda and Secretary hand
subtitle: Gothic-cursive hybrids that shaped medieval and Tudor writing
excerpt: Bastarda is the hybrid Gothic-cursive hand that dominated administrative and literary manuscripts in 14th- and 15th-century France, Flanders, and England. Secretary hand is its English administrative descendant, used on legal and commercial documents from roughly 1500 to 1700. Both demand a Mitchell Roundhand nib at a shallower angle than textura quadrata, and a willingness to read unfamiliar letterforms before practising them.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on historical scripts: public domain, Internet Archive scan available. Johnston's analysis of Gothic hands provides the structural framework used here. Alfred Fairbank, A Handwriting Manual (1932) is still in copyright (Fairbank died 1982; life + 70 applies in the UK); this entry draws on structural description only, not quotation. General palaeographical documentation of Secretary hand from Hector's The Handwriting of English Documents (1966) is referenced structurally; no direct quotation.

glossaryTerms.definition: A hybrid Gothic-cursive hand developed in 14th-century France and Flanders, combining the compressed body of textura with looped ascenders and cursive connecting strokes. The word describes the mixed parentage of the script.
The English administrative hand derived from Bastarda, used on legal, commercial, and personal documents from roughly 1500 to 1700. Notorious for its contracted letterforms and ambiguous e and r.
A single vertical downstroke in a Gothic or blackletter hand. Letters like m, n, u, and i are composed entirely of minims with entry and exit strokes.

The prescribed sequence of strokes used to build a letter, including the direction, order, and angle of each stroke. Getting the ductus right produces consistent letterforms; reversing or skipping strokes produces inconsistency.

An ascender stroke that curves back on itself to form a closed loop before the main body of the letter. A key feature distinguishing Bastarda and Secretary hand from the diamond-topped ascenders of textura quadrata.

content.text: Bastarda spread through French, Flemish, and English workshops in the 14th and 15th centuries. It combines the tight vertical forms of
bastarda
's textura ancestry with faster, more cursive strokes. The looped ascenders set it apart.
Secretary hand

is the English version of this script, in use from about 1500 to 1700. It shares most Bastarda letterforms with a few extra ambiguities. This tutorial covers the Bastarda alphabet first, then maps the key differences in Secretary hand.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, 3 (medium)
items.substitutions: Mitchell 2 for larger practice; Mitchell 4 for smaller

items.name: Iron-gall ink (modern formulation)
items.substitutions: Sumi ink works; avoid fountain-pen ink, which is too thin

items.name: Cartridge paper, 220 gsm
items.substitutions: 130 gsm cartridge for practice; 220 gsm for finished work

items.name: Ruling pen or HB pencil
items.substitutions: A sharp pencil works; a ruling pen gives cleaner guide lines

content.text: The move

Rule your guide lines. Bastarda works at 5 nib-widths of x-height. Stack the nib sideways five times between the baseline and x-height line to confirm the spacing. Add ascender and descender zones of roughly 3-4 nib-widths each. Use the ruling pen or a sharp pencil; lines that are too dark will show through the ink and distract.

Set the nib angle at 15 to 20 degrees from the horizontal baseline. This is shallower than the 30 degrees used for Foundational and noticeably shallower than the 45 degrees of textura quadrata. Place the pen on a scrap piece and draw a short horizontal stroke to verify: the thick-to-thin transition should show broad horizontals and thin diagonals. If the diagonals feel too thick, rotate the nib fractionally counter-clockwise.

Hold the pen between the index and middle fingers, with the thumb supporting from below, the hand resting on the side of the little finger or a sheet of tracing paper laid over the practice area. The grip is relaxed: the nib should glide on the downstroke without pressing into the paper surface.
Begin with the
minim

stroke, which is the building block of n, m, u, and i. The Bastarda minim has a small, angled entry hairline at the top, then a firm downstroke, then a short cursive exit that curves gently right before lifting. Practise 10 rows of isolated minims before attempting a letter. Consistency here controls the whole hand: irregular minim width or uneven spacing creates a page that vibrates rather than reads.

Work through the lowercase alphabet in groups of shared strokes. Start with i, u, n, m (minim letters). Then a, d, g, q (bowl letters with a descending stem or counter-clockwise bowl). Then b, h, k, l (letters with ascenders). Then e, o, c (round-bodied letters). Finish with f, p, r, s, t, v, w, x, y, z (the remaining forms).
The
looped ascender

distinguishes Bastarda immediately from textura. On b, d, h, k, and l, the ascender rises to roughly 3 nib-widths above the x-height, then curves back to the left and comes down to close the loop before rejoining the main stem. The loop does not need to close tightly: a partial loop or a hairline connection is correct. Do not force a perfectly round loop. The
ductus
is: up-stroke forming the loop, down the left side of the loop, then the main downstroke of the ascender, then the body of the letter.

The forked ascender, found on certain variant forms of b and l, is an alternative to the loop: the ascender rises, the nib lifts briefly and makes a short diagonal down-left, creating a fork at the top. Both forms are historically attested; choose one and use it consistently within a piece.

The Bastarda e is written with a high, narrow counter. The pen enters from the left at the x-height, curves right and up, then comes back left and closes. Unlike the open roman e, the Bastarda e often has a very small aperture. Practice this letter slowly until the closing stroke feels natural at speed.

Once the Bastarda lowercase is stable, map the key Secretary hand differences. The Secretary e has a high-stroke entry that crosses the top of the letter before the bowl is formed. The Secretary r, instead of the two-stroke r of Foundational, has a characteristic short diagonal dash off the stem at shoulder height. The Secretary a may resemble a closed loop rather than the open double-stroke bowl of roman hands. Work through a side-by-side comparison of Bastarda and Secretary letterforms before attempting Secretary on a practice sheet.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Most early Bastarda issues come from the wrong nib angle or rushing the looped ascenders.

items.symptom: The ascender loops look like rounded humps, not loops
items.cause: The pen is following a round path rather than closing back down the left side of the loop. The counter-clockwise curve needs to come back far enough to form a proper enclosed or nearly enclosed space.
items.fix: Slow down the loop stroke and exaggerate the leftward return. Over-loop until the shape is right, then reduce to the correct size.

items.cause: The nib angle is too steep. At 15-20 degrees, the pen produces strong contrast on the verticals. A 30-degree angle produces the Foundational ratio; a 45-degree angle produces the textura ratio. Both are too steep for Bastarda.
items.fix: Check the nib angle again with a short horizontal test stroke. Adjust the angle counter-clockwise if the horizontals are not the thickest element.

items.cause: Each minim in a letter like m or n has a slightly different width or entry angle.
items.fix: Drill the single minim stroke until the entry hairline and exit curve are automatic. Then write isolated n and m before using them in words.

items.symptom: Secretary hand e and r are unrecognisable
items.cause: The letterforms are quite unlike their roman equivalents. This is not a technique failure.
items.fix: Find a good facsimile of a 16th- or 17th-century English document and spend 15 minutes simply reading it. The eye learns to recognise Secretary letterforms before the hand can reliably produce them.

content.text: Where it leads

Bastarda and Secretary hand sit between formal blackletter and humanist Italic. Practising Bastarda makes the textura structure clear. Secretary hand opens a large archive of English documents. Tudor letters and parish registers become readable once you know the forms.
